title: Murder Mystery

jokes.text: Jocelyn warns the Jazzigals there is 'a murderer on the loose' in town, delivered with casual concern as a warm-up announcement
jokes.setup: Jocelyn opens warm-up with a routine-sounding community announcement about a murderer being on the loose in town
jokes.punchline: It's just Twyla's murder mystery party — the threat was fake all along
jokes.explanation: A clean cold-open misdirect — the casual, community-announcement delivery of 'there is a murderer on the loose' is the joke. Structurally solid: the panic-then-reveal lands well. Not wildly original but well-executed for an opening beat.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I need to warn you all about something very serious. There's a murderer in our community, and we need to be extremely careful.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: It's for my murder mystery party this weekend!

jokes.text: Twyla: 'Until then, it shall remain a mystery!' — theatrical pause before revealing it's a murder mystery party
jokes.setup: Twyla says she needs friends to catch the murderer, and that until then 'it shall remain a mystery'
jokes.punchline: She was literally just doing a dramatic bit for her party invitation — the theatrics were the tell
jokes.explanation: Twyla's earnest melodrama is perfectly in character — she's committed to the bit with complete sincerity. The 'it shall remain a mystery' line is a nice double meaning. Works because Twyla would absolutely do this.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, a murder mystery party is hardly our first foray into elaborate social events. We've attended far more... exclusive gatherings. Why, I once went to a party where everyone wore masks and very little else. It was very Eyes Wide Shut.

jokes.text: Moira mentions she and John used to attend 'Eyes Wide Shut' parties at the Castros'
jokes.setup: Moira responds warmly to the murder mystery invitation by referencing their social history
jokes.punchline: The reference is to an orgy-themed Kubrick film — casually revealing the Roses' decadent former lifestyle as contrast to a small-town party
jokes.explanation: The 'Eyes Wide Shut' reference is perfectly Moira — dropped with total nonchalance as if this were the most normal comparison in the world. The contrast between masked orgy parties and a small-town murder mystery potluck is rich, and the character integration is excellent. A savvy audience will immediately clock the implication.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: We have plans Saturday night.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Oh. I just guessed the day wrong.

jokes.text: Twyla: 'I don't know, it can get pretty wild.' / '...Or when!'
jokes.setup: Moira implies that Twyla's party will be 'somewhat different' from orgy parties
jokes.punchline: Twyla earnestly defends her party's wildness, and adds 'Or when!' about when it will end — which later we learn is because she wanders around winking without picking a murderer
jokes.explanation: The 'or when' is the stronger beat — Twyla's sincerity about her chaotic parties is very on-brand. Gets a rewatch bonus because Jocelyn's later story about Twyla forgetting to pick a murderer retroactively deepens the joke.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I appreciate the invitation, but I find myself in a position where I must respectfully table this matter pending a comprehensive review of my scheduling infrastructure and a full risk assessment of the event parameters.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Oh, I would be pleased to RSVP as... pending.'
jokes.setup: Twyla asks if Moira is coming, and Moira builds up to an enthusiastic-seeming RSVP
jokes.punchline: After a dramatic pause across a scene break, the answer is just 'pending' — an absurdly corporate non-answer delivered with theatrical weight
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. The structure is near-perfect: the scene cut between 'I would be pleased to RSVP as...' and '...pending' (with a timestamp gap at 01:16) creates a beautiful comedic pause that maximizes the deflation. 'Pending' as a social RSVP is absurdly corporate, and Moira would absolutely use it. High quotability, high character integration. Just below the 8.0 anchor level — very close.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We're going to position the Rosebud as a luxury destination. We'll offer premium amenities, impeccable service, an elevated experience.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Which luxury motel are we talking about?

jokes.text: Scene cut: Moira says 'I would be pleased to RSVP as...' and then cuts to her saying '...pending. Just uh, as soon as I double check, and circle back.'
jokes.setup: The scene cuts mid-sentence, creating anticipation for what should be a yes or no answer
jokes.punchline: The visual of Moira standing there having apparently deliberated at length, only to deliver 'pending' plus corporate jargon ('circle back'), is its own separate laugh from the word itself
jokes.explanation: The 'circle back' extension of the joke is a second comedic beat within the same moment. Moira treating an informal party RSVP with the language of corporate email is perfectly in character and gets its own laugh.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Well, I mean, I do look like a professional female golfer.

jokes.text: Johnny excitedly tells Stevie someone 'might' book a room, and keeps using the word 'might' to her increasing exasperation
jokes.setup: Stevie asks if someone booked a room. Johnny corrects her — 'Even better, someone MIGHT book a room.'
jokes.punchline: Stevie immediately points out 'How is that better?' and Johnny keeps using the word 'might' while insisting it's great news
jokes.explanation: A good character dynamic joke — Johnny's relentless optimism vs. Stevie's dry pragmatism. The logical flaw (might = better) is the engine of the joke and Stevie's deadpan 'How is that better?' is the perfect straight-man response.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You can drive the golf cart.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Can I drink?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Okay, I'm in.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Even better, someone might book a room.' / 'A lot of people might book a room if this goes through.'
jokes.setup: Stevie asks if someone booked a room — standard motel business.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's counter-offer of 'even better, someone MIGHT book a room' — the bar is subterranean, and he's genuinely thrilled about it.
jokes.explanation: Johnny's optimistic spin on deeply modest circumstances is a reliable character beat. Stevie's skepticism as the straight man is well-deployed. Functional character comedy that reveals both their relationship and the motel's situation efficiently.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You know, I've learned that in business, you have to be willing to do things that make you uncomfortable. Like, really uncomfortable. Like, gag reflex uncomfortable.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's... not the philosophy I was hoping to hear.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Well, that's the one you're getting. Because at the end of the day, money doesn't care about your feelings, Johnny. It only cares about your willingness to debase yourself.

jokes.text: Johnny pitches the golf package deal: 'on a $250 dollar package, a cool $40 goes into our pockets.' Stevie: 'That sounds like a better deal for them.'
jokes.setup: Johnny enthusiastically explains the financial terms of the golf partnership as if it's a great deal
jokes.punchline: Stevie immediately and correctly identifies that $40 out of $250 is a terrible return for the motel
jokes.explanation: The math joke lands because Stevie says exactly what the audience is thinking. Johnny's oblivious enthusiasm against Stevie's plain-spoken accounting is a reliable comedy engine for this show.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Are you coming to the party?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A party? Oh, I'd love to, but I'm famished. Could you have them prepare me a BLT?

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: No, I meant—

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: And hold the bread. I'm watching my carbs.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'It's all about building bridges, Stevie. But here's the thing, even if this deal falls through, we still get an afternoon on a golf course!' Stevie: '"We?"'
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to spin the deal's upside as 'building bridges' and getting to play golf
jokes.punchline: Stevie immediately catches that he's using 'we' to rope her into golf she never agreed to
jokes.explanation: Stevie's single-word response — just 'We?' in quotes — is economically perfect. It does everything: calls out Johnny's manipulation, reveals the dynamic, and gets a laugh with maximum efficiency. A textbook deadpan beat.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: I was really hoping you'd bring something special to the party.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Of course I'm bringing something special. I'm Moira Rose.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: It's potluck.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A potluck? Twyla, I don't do potluck. I do pot roast. I do pot pie. I do not do potluck.

jokes.text: Stevie's aside about a haircut in high school that made her look like she was in the LPGA
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to convince Stevie to play golf; she says it's not really her thing
jokes.punchline: She then provides an oddly specific exception: a high school haircut that people said made her look like she was in the LPGA
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'LPGA haircut' is what makes this funny — it's the kind of offhand, real-feeling personal detail that Stevie's character delivers well. Slightly unexpected, slightly self-deprecating, and tonally perfect for her.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: No one is coming.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Well, maybe they're all just busy. Or maybe they don't know where the venue is. Or maybe they think it's next weekend. Or maybe they all died.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'I might even let you drive the cart.' Stevie: 'Can you drink on the cart?' Johnny: 'Yeah.' Stevie: 'Well, I will get the "back in 15 minutes" sign.'
jokes.setup: Johnny offers Stevie the incentive of driving the cart to get her to come
jokes.punchline: Stevie's only condition is drinking, and the moment she hears yes she's completely on board — immediately going for the 'closed' sign
jokes.explanation: Perfect Stevie joke. The beat of her asking about drinking, getting the confirmation, and immediately abandoning all pretense of work responsibility is swift and highly character-specific. The 'back in 15 minutes' sign is the visual button that sells it.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Well, I mean, if Brenda's cousin's friend can get off work, and if the weather is nice, and if my car doesn't break down again, then maybe we'll have a really good turnout.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Well, it's gonna take longer than 15 minutes.' / Stevie: 'You have to give people hope.'
jokes.setup: Stevie gets the '15 minutes' sign despite knowing the golf afternoon will take much longer
jokes.punchline: 'You have to give people hope' — Stevie's philosophy of managing customer expectations with cheerful deception
jokes.explanation: A great line. 'You have to give people hope' delivered deadpan as a business philosophy about lying to customers is both funny and revealing of Stevie's character. High quotability — it works as a standalone maxim with a darkly comic subtext.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Well, I do have some guests coming. I have a pathetic guest list that's basically my mom's dating situation.

jokes.text: Moira orders 'BLT please, no bread' when Twyla asks if she's coming to the party
jokes.setup: Twyla says she doesn't mean to pressure Moira but is anxious to know if she's coming
jokes.punchline: Moira responds as if she's being asked for her lunch order — 'BLT please, no bread' — completely missing or ignoring the question
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. Moira's complete pivot to her food order is perfectly timed and deeply character-specific — she's not being rude intentionally, she's just operating on an entirely different plane of awareness. The 'no bread' addition to a BLT is itself a small absurdist joke within the joke (a BLT without bread is just lettuce and tomato). Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You cannot comp influential guests. It sends the wrong message.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: But Moira, it's free anyway. We're not losing money.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Free?! Oh my God, that's even worse. Now they think we're desperate.

jokes.text: Moira asks what 'chockablock' means in terms of Friday — stalling with nonsense vocabulary to avoid committing to the party
jokes.setup: Moira says the end of the week is 'chockablock' as an excuse not to commit
jokes.punchline: The word 'chockablock' is itself absurd — Moira's elevated vocabulary used as a dodge is a running character trait
jokes.explanation: A mild character beat — Moira's use of archaic vocabulary ('chockablock') as social armor is on-brand. Not a strong standalone joke but contributes to her characterization.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, David, there's a man. He just... he comes to the house. Every day. With letters.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, that's the mailman.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: But why? Why does he do that? Is he being held captive? Does he need help?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, Alexis. It's his job.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: His job is to come into our house uninvited and leave things? That's either a job or a crime, David, and I'm not sure which.

jokes.text: Twyla: 'I was actually thinking bagel bites.' — after Moira implies she'd bring a touch of celebrity
jokes.setup: Twyla tells Moira she was hoping Moira would 'bring a little something to the party — a touch of celebrity, perhaps'
jokes.punchline: Twyla thought she literally meant food — bagel bites specifically — not cachet or star power
jokes.explanation: Perfectly Twyla. The innocent literalism — taking Moira's metaphorical 'bring something to the party' as a potluck request — is a beautifully simple misdirect. 'Bagel bites' as the specific choice is exactly right: humble, earnest, and completely deflating to Moira's grandeur.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, so he just... comes to our house every day?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: And he brings us things?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: For free?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, no. I'm sure there's a fee involved somewhere. No one does anything for free.

jokes.text: Moira learning it's a potluck: 'Oh, you most certainly did not' (tell me it was a potluck)
jokes.setup: Twyla mentions it's a potluck after Moira is already partially committed
jokes.punchline: Moira's theatrical indignation — 'You most certainly did not' — at not being warned is out of proportion to the offense
jokes.explanation: A functional character comedy beat — Moira's horror at mundane party logistics. Not especially original but serves the scene's rhythm.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So like, how much do you usually tip the mailman? Because I've been doing like fifty bucks a week.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Alexis, you don't tip the mailman. He's a federal employee. The post office is funded by taxes.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, so I've just been giving money to a stranger?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes. Although, now that I'm saying it out loud, I'm not entirely sure how the postal service works.

jokes.text: Twyla: 'Well, I would ask someone else, it's just that uh, so far no one is coming.'
jokes.setup: Twyla is trying to persuade Moira to come to her party
jokes.punchline: She reveals that literally nobody has RSVP'd — delivered with Twyla's characteristic gentle matter-of-factness
jokes.explanation: Twyla's guileless delivery of catastrophically bad news is very funny. She says 'so far no one is coming' with the same tone she'd use to describe mild weather. The understatement is the joke.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You're always hanging around here. Don't you have a job?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm not hanging around, David. I'm the PR consultant for the Woebegone Clothing Company.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That closed down three months ago.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, I'm also dating Ted now, so I have a lot going on.

jokes.text: Twyla: 'Well, in fairness, the party is still a few hours away.' — after Moira points out no one is coming
jokes.setup: Moira is alarmed that no one is coming to Twyla's party
jokes.punchline: Twyla defends herself by noting the party is still hours away — technically correct but deeply optimistic given the circumstances
jokes.explanation: Twyla's relentless good-natured optimism in the face of social failure is one of the show's warmest comedy veins. 'The party is still a few hours away' as a defense of zero RSVPs is quietly hilarious — it's the logic of someone who cannot conceive of failure.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You're taking astronomy?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yeah, I thought it would be like, astrology, you know? Like reading people's signs and stuff.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's astrology, not astronomy.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, I didn't know that! I thought it was like a really exclusive thing, like, you know, only beautiful people could do it.

jokes.text: Twyla's escalating defense: 'My mom will come. And she'll probably bring whoever she's dating. And he'll most likely bring some younger friend.'
jokes.setup: Moira presses Twyla on having no guest list; Twyla insists she said 'no one' too hastily
jokes.punchline: She builds a guest list from her own mother, to her mother's date, to that date's 'younger friend' — each step more tenuous and revealing of Twyla's lonely reality
jokes.explanation: A lovely piece of escalating character comedy. The three-step logic — mom, mom's boyfriend, boyfriend's younger friend — reveals a lot about Twyla's world while being genuinely funny. 'He'll most likely bring some younger friend' implies a whole world of slightly seedy backstory without spelling it out. Rewatch bonus for the depth.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Now, you want to grip it firmly, but not too tight. You gotta feel it in your hands.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Okay.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: And then you're gonna want to spread your legs a little wider for better balance.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Right.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Now bend over and really get your rear end up in the air.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I'm not doing that.

jokes.text: Moira: 'You'd be shocked at how many celebrities show up to a thing with the promise of a free Wagyu slider.'
jokes.setup: Moira launches into party-boosting tactics — starting with 'comping a few influential guests'
jokes.punchline: Her insider knowledge of celebrity behavior (Wagyu sliders as the currency of fame) is absurdly specific and delivered as genuine wisdom
jokes.explanation: The joke works because Moira is completely sincere — she's applying her experience of A-list Hollywood events to a small-town murder mystery potluck. 'Wagyu slider' as the tipping point for celebrity attendance is a perfect detail. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Okay, now spread your legs a little wider. No, wider. Come on, don't be shy.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, this is getting very uncomfortable.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Just trust me. Now bend over and grab your ankles.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm not doing that.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Johnny, I'm trying to teach you golf here. Now turn around and let me get behind you to show you the proper grip.

jokes.text: Twyla: 'I-I'm not charging anyone, this is just for fun.' Moira: 'Oh, this is worse than I assumed!'
jokes.setup: Moira tells Twyla to 'comp' influential guests; Twyla reveals she isn't charging anyone
jokes.punchline: Moira's horrified 'This is worse than I assumed!' — treating a free-party as a deeper crisis than a poorly-attended one
jokes.explanation: Moira's horror at the free party is a great encapsulation of her worldview — events without financial stakes are somehow more chaotic. 'This is worse than I assumed' is a highly quotable reaction beat that only Moira would deliver.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: These golf grips are ridiculously expensive. What makes them worth the price?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, they've got names like the Velvet Hammer, the Sultan's Caress, the Gentleman's Touch...

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Those aren't real grip names, are they?

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: No, but they sound expensive, don't they?

jokes.text: David's amazement that mail still exists: 'Did you know you can still get mail? No, but I'm talking about like, mail, mail. Like a little man in a uniform, with his satchel full of letters, traveling door to door.'
jokes.setup: Alexis finds David checking the mail; David is oddly fascinated by the concept
jokes.punchline: David describes postal mail as if he's discovered an anthropological curiosity — treating something completely normal as a revelation
jokes.explanation: David's sheltered-wealthy-person obliviousness is on full display. The running description of a mailman as 'a little man with a satchel' is funny because of the earnest anthropological tone — he's treating a mailman like a new species. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Are we... are we rolling right now?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, we're rolling.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Oh God. Um, okay. Just so you know, I'm not really comfortable being on camera.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Are you kidding me? This is fantastic! I'm a natural in front of the lens!

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Roland, you're not even in frame.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Not yet. Just wait.

jokes.text: Alexis corrects David that mail delivery is free; David says 'I don't think so...'
jokes.setup: David implies you pay the mailman directly, like a service
jokes.punchline: When Alexis says it's free delivery, David refuses to believe it — both siblings are equally out of touch with basic life
jokes.explanation: The joke is doubled — not just David not knowing mail is free, but both Roses being equally ignorant. It's a light but solid character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Jocelyn, darling, I haven't received your RSVP. Has something dreadful happened? Have you been struck by tragedy? Is it a death in the family? A debilitating illness? A social scandal of such magnitude that you can no longer show your face in polite society?

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: No, I just... I didn't see the invitation.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, that IS a tragedy.

jokes.text: David: 'How far into the 15 minutes do you think we are?'
jokes.setup: David and Alexis are waiting for Stevie to return; the 'back in 15 minutes' sign has been up
jokes.punchline: David asks this as if the timeline is genuinely mysterious — he has nowhere to be and nothing to do, which is itself the joke
jokes.explanation: A light callback with good economy. The implication that David has been idly waiting and genuinely wondering about the 15-minute ETA reveals his complete vacancy of purpose. Works as a callback because audience remembers the 15-minute joke.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Twyla forgot to assign the murderer role

jokes.text: Alexis: 'You've just been kind of hanging around a lot lately, which isn't a bad thing... but it's also not a good thing.'
jokes.setup: Alexis gently tries to address David's aimless loitering at the motel
jokes.punchline: Her diplomatic framing — 'not a bad thing... but also not a good thing' — is a perfectly useless piece of feedback that still manages to land
jokes.explanation: Alexis's diplomatic non-verdict is very well-written. The structure of 'not a bad thing... but also not a good thing' is funnily useless — it says nothing while pretending to be thoughtful. Highlights how both siblings have grown slightly more self-aware while still being deeply self-involved.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: So then we organized this murder mystery party, right? And someone actually stole the silverware.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I mean, we were all so into character, nobody realized it was a real crime until the next morning.

jokes.text: Alexis clarifies Ted is not her ex-boyfriend but her 'ex-fiancé'
jokes.setup: David calls Ted Alexis's 'ex-boyfriend'
jokes.punchline: Alexis corrects him — ex-fiancé — with the pride of someone who considers that a status upgrade, despite it arguably being worse
jokes.explanation: A quick character beat that rewards viewers who know Alexis's history. Her insistence on the proper title speaks to her self-image.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The party has really picked up bodies.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: That sounds... positive?

jokes.text: Alexis circled 'Stars and Cosmology' on a college brochure thinking it said 'Cosmetology'
jokes.setup: David notices Alexis has circled a course; Alexis says she's broadening her horizons
jokes.punchline: 'You've circled Stars and Cosmology?' / 'Oh yeah, I thought it said Cosmetology.'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The setup is perfect — Alexis earnestly talking about 'broadening her horizons' — and the punchline is beautifully in character. Cosmology vs. cosmetology is a malapropism that only Alexis would make, and her complete lack of embarrassment about the mix-up makes it funnier. High character integration, clean structure, great quotability. Nearly reaches 8.0 anchor territory.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I lost my diploma.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You lost your diploma?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I don't know where it is. It was probably in one of my bags that I left in the Hamptons, or it could be in a storage unit somewhere. I really don't know.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, this is just adding insult to injury.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You know, I wish that our financial ruin was a natural disaster. At least then we'd have something to blame other than ourselves.

jokes.text: Roland arrives with Jocelyn's golf visor and glove, offering to give Johnny golf tips 'right here on Johnny'
jokes.setup: Roland shows up unsolicited with golf equipment and decides to use Johnny as a demonstration model
jokes.punchline: Johnny's increasing discomfort as Roland physically adjusts his stance, while Stevie watches with delight
jokes.explanation: The Roland-as-unwanted-golf-coach bit is a reliable cringe comedy setup. Johnny's polite resistance while Roland steamrolls him is a good dynamic, though not the sharpest execution of this kind of joke.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I'm sure they won't reject you just for a missing diploma.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: They literally just did.

jokes.text: Roland: 'pop the old tush... Just go, Bam!' — giving Johnny unsolicited and overly physical golf instruction
jokes.setup: Roland is demonstrating golf stance adjustments to Johnny and Stevie
jokes.punchline: 'Pop the old tush... Bam!' — Roland's folksy, physical instructions create an increasingly awkward physical encounter between the two men
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy derived from Roland's oblivious invasion of Johnny's personal space. 'Pop the old tush' is funny in isolation — the folksy phrasing vs. Johnny's stiff formality — though the extended sequence is more cringe than sharp wit.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What are you doing?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm organizing the store inventory system using a color-coded filing method that accounts for seasonal fluctuations, supplier lead times, and customer preference patterns. It's not just filing, Alexis. It's a comprehensive logistics solution.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Well, I've been golfing for 30 years, Roland.' Roland: 'Wow, I like that.' — before immediately dismissing his technique
jokes.setup: Johnny defends his stance by citing 30 years of experience
jokes.punchline: Roland says 'I like that' before immediately saying there's one thing he doesn't like — invalidating the entire defense
jokes.explanation: The fake validation — 'I like that' immediately followed by criticism — is a classic comedic structure executed well here. Roland's complete lack of self-awareness about the contradiction is very in character.

dialogue.speaker: Golf Club Employee
dialogue.line: Where did you play this morning?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Uh... Pebble Beach.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: No, we played... Shadow Creek.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, Shadow Creek at Pebble Beach.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Are you using the Vargon grip?' Roland: 'I'm using La Va Grande.' — two made-up golf grips debated with total confidence
jokes.setup: Johnny and Roland begin competing over who knows more about golf technique
jokes.punchline: Both cite made-up (or highly obscure) grip names with complete authority — 'Vargon grip' and 'La Va Grande' — as if these are universally understood terms
jokes.explanation: The absurdist specificity of made-up golf terminology, delivered with complete conviction by both men, is genuinely funny. The escalation of their mutual posturing, each inventing fancier-sounding technique names, is well-calibrated.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We played at, uh... Pebble Beach.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: No, we said Torrey Pines.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Right, yes, Torrey Pines. Very exclusive.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: In... Canada.

jokes.text: Stevie secretly films Roland and Johnny's golf lesson on her phone; Johnny demands she delete it; Roland asks for a copy
jokes.setup: Stevie watches the awkward golf demonstration and quietly starts recording it
jokes.punchline: Johnny is horrified; Roland immediately requests a copy — revealing he has zero self-awareness about how ridiculous they look
jokes.explanation: The three-way reaction — Stevie's gleeful recording, Johnny's panic, Roland's enthusiastic request for a copy — efficiently captures all three characters with one beat. Roland's total absence of embarrassment is the best part.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's a very exclusive course. You wouldn't have heard of it.

dialogue.speaker: Golf Manager
dialogue.line: Where is it?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's in... the Hamptons.

dialogue.speaker: Golf Manager
dialogue.line: Which Hamptons course?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's very private. They don't advertise. Very selective membership.

jokes.text: Moira: 'it seems our mayor is keeping summer hours' — delivered dryly when Jocelyn asks if Roland is still there
jokes.setup: Jocelyn looks for Roland at the motel and Moira informs her he's not there
jokes.punchline: 'Our mayor is keeping summer hours' — a polished dig at Roland's laziness dressed up in euphemism
jokes.explanation: 'Keeping summer hours' as a euphemism for the mayor simply not doing his job is a sharp, economical insult. Very Moira — refined vocabulary used as a scalpel. High quotability as a standalone phrase.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's a very exclusive course. Extremely exclusive. You wouldn't know about it because... it's not open to the public. It's private. Very private.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: The fairways are maintained to the highest standard. Immaculate, really. Championship-level maintenance.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's just... it's not widely advertised because they prefer to keep membership limited. Word of mouth only. Very discreet operation.

jokes.text: Jocelyn's account of last year's murder mystery: Twyla 'wandered around kind of winking at everybody, until she realized she hadn't picked a murderer'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn is explaining why she doesn't want to go to Twyla's party — it's not great
jokes.punchline: Last year's party ended at 3am because Twyla was winking at everyone without having actually assigned a murderer — the party had no conclusion by design flaw
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's strongest joke. The image of Twyla wandering a party for hours winking mysteriously at everyone, while secretly having forgotten to designate a murderer, is a perfect piece of character comedy. It's specific, absurd, deeply in character, and retroactively enriches Twyla's earlier comment about parties not ending. A strong 8 — original, well-structured, and completely character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I was just thinking about all those crazy things we used to do in college. Like throwing the sack around.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Wait, you threw sex around in college?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: No, the sack. The football sack.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, that is so much less interesting.

jokes.text: Jocelyn: 'And then the year before there were three murderers, and one of them actually robbed the cafe!'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn continues cataloguing reasons why Twyla's murder mystery parties are disasters
jokes.punchline: The previous year had three murderers (already absurd), and one of them actually committed a real robbery during the game — escalating into real crime
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's single best joke. The escalation from 'three murderers' (already a great absurdist image — a murder mystery game where a third of the party is the murderer) to 'one of them actually robbed the cafe' is a perfect dark punchline. The joke works on multiple levels: it's a commentary on Twyla's chaotic party-planning, a callback to small-town lawlessness, and an absurdist escalation that lands with real surprise. Genuinely exceptional. Comfortably above the 8.0 anchor.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Wait, you didn't actually go to UCLA?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, I mean, I was *accepted*. I just didn't actually attend. But that's basically the same thing.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: That is not the same thing at all.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, but I'm very knowledgeable about Los Angeles. I could have gone. I just chose not to, which I think is even more impressive.

jokes.text: Moira: 'it's no less than a snowball gathering speed, picking up bodies as it rolls downhill. And that's good? Very good.'
jokes.setup: Moira tries to inspire Jocelyn to join the party with a motivational metaphor
jokes.punchline: Her metaphor for success involves a snowball 'picking up bodies' — an image that sounds violent; Jocelyn has to ask if that's good
jokes.explanation: Moira's dark, unintentionally violent metaphor ('picking up bodies') is a wonderful character beat — she chooses words that sound menacing while meaning something positive. Jocelyn's confused 'And that's good?' and Moira's 'Very good' is a perfect two-beat exchange. Quintessential Moira.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I took a semester off.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You took a semester off? For what?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, I was going to go back, but then I ran into Kourtney Kardashian at Nobu, and I just felt like, you know, why sit in a lecture hall when you could be networking with celebrities?

jokes.text: Moira: 'Be the snowball. Be the snowball!' — repeated as an inspirational send-off
jokes.setup: Moira builds to an inspirational crescendo after her body-collecting snowball metaphor
jokes.punchline: The absurdity of 'Be the snowball' as a life motto, delivered twice with full theatrical commitment
jokes.explanation: The repetition of 'Be the snowball' is itself the joke — Moira's absolute conviction that this dark metaphor is inspiring. It's a running gag within a single scene, and the second 'Be the snowball' lands harder than the first. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I followed the American Idol tour instead of finishing school.

jokes.text: Alexis on the phone trying to get into college without her diploma: 'No, I wish it was a flood... Okay well, chasing down the paperwork seems like a lot of work.'
jokes.setup: Alexis is calling the college trying to explain why she can't produce her high school diploma
jokes.punchline: She apparently tried to claim it was destroyed in a flood — and when they don't buy it, she just decides the paperwork is too much work
jokes.explanation: We only hear Alexis's side of the call, but the implication is clear: she tried to invent a flood story. Her pivot to 'chasing down paperwork seems like a lot of work' when the lie fails is perfectly Alexis — she expected to charm her way through and is genuinely surprised it didn't work.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I'm ready to go hit some golf sticks.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: They're called golf clubs.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: That's what I said. Golf sticks.

jokes.text: David's reaction when Alexis reveals she's looking at courses: 'Okay, this is sort of my thing, though.'
jokes.setup: Alexis announces she's thinking of taking courses
jokes.punchline: David claims 'education' as his personal domain — despite showing zero evidence of pursuing it
jokes.explanation: David territorially claiming 'education' as 'his thing' while lying on a bed doing nothing is a classic sibling dynamic joke. The gap between his claim and his behavior is the source of the humor. Economy is excellent — three words do all the work.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Did you know that a group of flamingos is called a 'flamboyance'? I just learned that. I have nothing to do.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'I really don't think you can claim education as your thing' — delivered with complete sincerity
jokes.setup: David claims education is 'sort of his thing'
jokes.punchline: Alexis, who circled Cosmology thinking it was Cosmetology, tells David he can't claim education — with total lack of self-awareness
jokes.explanation: The joke is enriched by the Cosmology callback — the audience knows Alexis's relationship with education is even shakier than David's. Her straight-faced rebuttal to him is funnier when you remember she can't tell Cosmology from Cosmetology. Rewatch bonus for this layering.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Did you know that honey never spoils? Archaeologists have found pots of honey in Egyptian tombs that are thousands of years old and still perfectly edible.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David, that is the saddest thing I've ever heard. You've really hit rock bottom, haven't you? Next you'll be telling me facts about the mating habits of seahorses.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'the stupid people at the college wouldn't just take my word for it when I told them that I had a high school diploma'
jokes.setup: Alexis is frustrated she can't get into the college courses
jokes.punchline: She's genuinely offended that they want documentation rather than her verbal assurance — this is completely normal to her
jokes.explanation: Alexis's bafflement that institutions require proof rather than her word is a great encapsulation of her privilege. It's a joke that reveals character while being funny — she truly cannot fathom why her word isn't sufficient.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David, darling, what you're experiencing is a severe case of ennui. It's a malaise of the spirit, a profound weariness of the soul that only the truly cultured and sophisticated can properly diagnose. You're not depressed, you're not tired—you're ennui-ed. It's far more elegant, really.

jokes.text: David: 'Well, it's not like they're gonna be like, "oh, you don't have your high school diploma, you can't come to our college."' Alexis: 'That's almost word for word what they just said to me on the phone.'
jokes.setup: David confidently reassures Alexis that colleges won't actually enforce the diploma requirement
jokes.punchline: Alexis reveals that's almost exactly what happened — David's dismissal is immediately and perfectly contradicted
jokes.explanation: A structurally perfect joke. David's confident dismissal of a real concern is immediately proven wrong in the most direct way possible — 'that's almost word for word what they said.' The structure (confident wrong prediction + immediate exact confirmation of the prediction) is very clean. David's obliviousness to real-world systems is a recurring theme and this is a great execution of it.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David, you have influence in this town. You cannot simply squander it on your various whims and complaints.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mother, I have the same influence as a Starbucks in a town with one Starbucks. It's not influence, it's lack of options.

jokes.text: David: 'Well... I just don't think I have that kind of time.' — after the conversation about college, immediately pivoting to
jokes.setup: The siblings have been discussing the difficulty of going back to school
jokes.punchline: David, who has been doing nothing all episode, claims he doesn't have time — the gap between his actual vacancy and his claimed busyness is the joke
jokes.explanation: Maximum economy — David's claim of not having 'that kind of time' while having done nothing all day is a perfect understatement. The implication is crystal clear and needs no elaboration.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David, you simply must attend this soirée. It will be the bee's knees, the cat's pajamas, the elephant's eyebrows!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mother, the elephant's eyebrows? That's not even a real phrase.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, it is now, and it's absolutely the berries!

jokes.text: David immediately pivots from the education conversation to: 'Okay, what are you doing? / Reorganizing my knits!'
jokes.setup: After claiming he's too busy for college, David is found reorganizing his knitwear collection
jokes.punchline: 'Reorganizing my knits!' delivered with the urgency and pride of someone doing important work
jokes.explanation: 'Reorganizing my knits!' is a great character line — delivered with total conviction, the activity itself is hilariously trivial for someone claiming to be busy. The exclamation point is load-bearing. Very David.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, where are you? You said you'd be here.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I know, but I've decided my work here is done.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Your work? You're not even here.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Exactly. I've successfully lowered everyone's expectations of me so much that my mere absence is now a gift.

jokes.text: Johnny and Stevie's fake golf course: 'It was down the road. Uh, Maple... / Grove? / Grove, Maple Grove.'
jokes.setup: The golf club managers ask where Johnny and Stevie were playing before — they've shown up in golf clothes but have never actually played
jokes.punchline: Johnny and Stevie fumble together to invent a fake golf course name in real time — 'Maple Grove' is assembled piece by piece
jokes.explanation: The collaborative lying — Johnny starting the name, Stevie supplying 'Grove,' Johnny accepting it — is a well-executed cringe moment. The callback that it's 'exclusive' and 'way back off the road' to explain why the manager hasn't heard of it adds a nice improvised cover-up layer.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, you're the murderer everyone's been looking for?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I didn't *plan* to be. But when life gives you an opportunity to disappear, you simply must take it. Besides, the lighting in that abandoned warehouse was absolutely dreadful. I'm doing them a favor by staying missing.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You can't just escape from a murder investigation!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Darling, I've escaped far worse. Three marriages, the nineties, and that unfortunate incident with the community theater production of Cats. This is nothing.

jokes.text: Johnny lists motel amenities including 'working toilets, which probably sounds like something I don't need to mention but you know, just covering all the bases'
jokes.setup: Johnny is pitching the motel's upgraded features to potential business partners
jokes.punchline: He includes 'working toilets' as a selling point — acknowledging mid-sentence that this is a very low bar to advertise
jokes.explanation: A great joke about the state of the motel — the fact that 'working toilets' has to be mentioned as an upgrade feature, and that Johnny knows it sounds bad but says it anyway, reveals exactly how dire the baseline is. The self-aware parenthetical ('which probably sounds like something I don't need to mention') is the clever twist.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, the party was a tremendous success. The ambiance, the flow, the way I orchestrated every single detail.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, you didn't do anything. You showed up twenty minutes late.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, but my presence elevated the entire evening. I was the life of the party.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You spent the whole time telling people about your colonoscopy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, nobody else was talking about theirs.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God. Wait. Were you the murderer in the murder mystery game?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: What? No. Well, yes, but—

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You were assigned the murderer and you've been taking credit for the party this whole time. Of course you were.

jokes.text: The rival motel has 'coin laundry, and a Jacuzzi' — killing Johnny's pitch
jokes.setup: Johnny has just enthusiastically pitched the Rose Motel's upgraded amenities
jokes.punchline: The competing Elmington Moto-Lodge already has coin laundry and a Jacuzzi — Johnny's pitch is instantly deflated by the very modest competition
jokes.explanation: The punchline here is that a coin laundry and Jacuzzi — modest amenities at any other motel — are enough to make Johnny's pitch obsolete. The comedy is in how low the bar is, and how far below it Johnny still sits.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We were there for your graduation, sweetheart. We saw the whole thing.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, we arrived early, got excellent seats, and we were very moved.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You left after fifteen minutes.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's not... we didn't leave early.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: There was an issue with the acoustics in the gymnasium.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You missed my entire name being called. You missed me walking across the stage. You missed everything.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, we got the gist of it.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Well, as an avid golfer myself...' — when he hasn't played in three years and came in golf attire as a disguise
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to leverage his claimed golf expertise to seem like a good partner for the golf club
jokes.punchline: The audience knows he hasn't played in three years, showed up in borrowed clothes, and just lied about a fake golf course — 'avid golfer' is a pure fabrication
jokes.explanation: Works as a callback joke — the audience is ahead of the golf club managers on this one. Johnny's brazen self-description as an 'avid golfer' lands harder because of all the prior context about his actual golf situation.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We didn't actually stay for the whole thing.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It was rather tedious.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We left after the first half hour.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The speeches were interminable.

jokes.text: Manager: 'Oh sure, might as well use some of the amenities.' [as Johnny goes to use their restroom after a failed pitch]
jokes.setup: Johnny excuses himself to use the restroom after being dismissed.
jokes.punchline: The manager's dry 'might as well use some of the amenities' implies: you got nothing else from us, you might as well take the bathroom.
jokes.explanation: A good button on the failed meeting scene. The manager's polite passive-aggression is sharp. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Look at this beautiful photo from Alexis's graduation.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Johnny, that's not Alexis.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What do you mean? Of course it is.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Dad, I've never seen that person in my life.

jokes.text: David's IMDB Rosa Parks discovery: 'Did you know that according to IMDB, Rosa Parks was in an episode of "Touched By an Angel?"'
jokes.setup: Moira walks in on David lying in bed doing nothing
jokes.punchline: The specific trivia David has landed on in his boredom — Rosa Parks' IMDB page — is both random and perfect. He presents it as a discovery of great importance.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'Rosa Parks on Touched By an Angel' is what makes this great — it's the kind of rabbit hole you only fall into during profound boredom, and David presents it with the gravity of an important finding. The joke is simultaneously about David's boredom, his lack of priorities, and the genuine absurdity of the trivia itself. High originality.

jokes.text: Moira: 'You are bored, lethargic, and practically dripping with ennui!'
jokes.setup: Moira diagnoses David's condition after finding him lying in bed with IMDB trivia
jokes.punchline: 'Practically dripping with ennui' — the escalating theatricality of the diagnosis, using a French word for boredom, is Moira at her most Moira
jokes.explanation: The phrase 'practically dripping with ennui' is a great Moira line — French vocabulary, physical metaphor, theatrical excess. It's exactly how she would describe ordinary boredom. Very high quotability and character integration.

jokes.text: David: 'I'm at the buffet, and there is nothing to taste!'
jokes.setup: Moira tells David he should be out 'tasting things' as a tastemaker
jokes.punchline: David extends the buffet metaphor — he's at the buffet (Schitt's Creek), there is nothing worth tasting (nothing appeals to him), therefore the fault lies with the town
jokes.explanation: David's buffet retort is a well-executed extension of Moira's metaphor — he picks it up and turns it into a complaint about the town, which is both funny and revelatory of his character in Season 3. Good economy and character-specific.

jokes.text: David's flat refusal: 'Fine, I will make a surprise appearance. But it's twice around the room, and then I'm out.'
jokes.setup: Moira convinces David to go to Twyla's murder mystery party
jokes.punchline: David agrees but sets extremely specific limitations — 'twice around the room, then I'm out' — as if it's a diplomatic visit requiring formal parameters
jokes.explanation: 'Twice around the room, then I'm out' is a very quotable, very David line. He treats a small-town party like a royal visit with formal protocols. The specificity of 'twice around' (not once, not three times) is the detail that sells it. High quotability.

jokes.text: Ted: 'We always hoped they'd lead to sex, but... still.'
jokes.setup: Ted has corrected David that they were hacky sack circles, not sex circles.
jokes.punchline: He then admits they always hoped the hacky sack circles would lead to sex, which partially validates David's interpretation.
jokes.explanation: A good callback button that validates the double entendre retroactively. 'Still.' as the trailing word does a lot — it implies resigned acceptance. Ted's earnest sexual hope is an unexpectedly relatable confession.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Is there any other way?' — responding to David's conditions
jokes.setup: David lays out his rigid conditions for attending the party
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'Is there any other way?' acknowledges that David's overcomplicated, self-important approach is simply his nature — she's not even surprised
jokes.explanation: A very economical button to the scene. Moira's weary acceptance of David's theatrics — 'Is there any other way?' — is both a joke about their relationship and a recognition that this IS David's way. Maximum economy.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Didn't you say on your resume that you did four years at UCLA?' / 'Yes... technically, if you consider Bel Air an extension of the campus, which everybody does.'
jokes.setup: David asks if Alexis has lied about having a degree on her resume
jokes.punchline: Alexis admits she listed UCLA on her resume but her 'attendance' was living in Bel Air — which she claims is essentially the same thing
jokes.explanation: A terrific joke. The logic of 'Bel Air is an extension of the UCLA campus, which everybody does' is completely Alexis — the breezy confidence of someone who has never had to justify anything before. It's specific (Bel Air/UCLA geography), character-perfect, and uses Alexis's particular brand of entitlement-logic flawlessly. High quotability and character integration.

jokes.text: David: 'Truth be told, it took me a minute to finish high school myself.' He and some friends followed the American Idol tour.
jokes.setup: David tries to make Alexis feel better about not finishing high school by sharing his own experience
jokes.punchline: He followed the American Idol tour with friends and missed school — then adds 'it's always the exact same set, so... no regrets'
jokes.explanation: David's attempt at solidarity is immediately undermined by the specificity of 'following the American Idol tour' — and his footnote ('it's always the exact same set, so no regrets') is a pitch-perfect button. The fact that this is supposed to be comforting makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Ted: 'We got some serious sack circles going.' Alexis: 'Oh, so many sex circles.' Ted: 'Sack. Hacky sack. We always hoped they'd lead to sex, but... still.'
jokes.setup: Ted talks about hacky sack circles; Alexis mishears 'sack' as something sexual
jokes.punchline: Ted clarifies it's hacky sack, then immediately admits they hoped hacky sack would lead to sex — a disarmingly honest confession that undercuts his wholesomeness
jokes.explanation: The double-take on 'sack' is a mild wordplay setup, but Ted's addendum — 'We always hoped they'd lead to sex, but... still' — is the real punchline. The confession is endearingly honest and slightly deflating to his straight-laced image. The pause before 'still' is key.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'I really wish you hadn't told me that last little bit, but... thank you.' David: 'That makes two of us.'
jokes.setup: David has just shared the American Idol tour story as a comfort
jokes.punchline: Both siblings immediately regret the story — Alexis explicitly, David by agreeing it was a mistake to share
jokes.explanation: A clean button to the American Idol bit. 'That makes two of us' from David is a good economy beat — he tried to comfort her, the story made it worse, and he acknowledges it without drama. The rhythm of the exchange is well-timed.

jokes.text: Alexis reveals she met Beyoncé in Mykonos as justification for taking a semester off and not finishing high school
jokes.setup: Alexis confesses she didn't finish high school because she took a semester off
jokes.punchline: 'But I did meet Beyoncé in Mykonos, so it was almost worth it' — the specific name-drop and location treat not finishing high school as a reasonable trade-off
jokes.explanation: Alexis's offhand Beyoncé-in-Mykonos justification for not finishing high school is a great character joke — it reveals her entire value system in one sentence. The hedging 'it was almost worth it' is the right note: even she's not entirely sure. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: David discovering Stevie and Johnny got onto the golf course: 'Let's get our golf sticks.'
jokes.setup: Stevie asked for tee time and got it through sheer directness
jokes.punchline: Johnny's excited call to action: 'Let's get our golf sticks' — calling them 'sticks' instead of clubs, revealing his actual relationship with the sport
jokes.explanation: A quiet joke — 'golf sticks' for someone who's supposedly been playing for 30 years and has a complex about being an 'avid golfer.' The malapropism in the moment of triumph is a light but well-placed character detail.

jokes.text: David: 'Okay, that's a bit much.' / Moira: 'I blame myself.'
jokes.setup: Moira has over-dramatically diagnosed David with ennui
jokes.punchline: David's mild pushback is immediately absorbed by Moira pivoting to self-blame — making the moment about her rather than him
jokes.explanation: The pivot to self-blame is a beautiful Moira move — it sounds like empathy but is actually narcissism. She's not comforting David, she's centering herself. The speed of the pivot from his objection to her martyrdom is excellent.

jokes.text: Moira's speech about Roaring Twenties dress: 'don't be a dew-dropper! Throw some concealer under those peepers, make like a swell, and go put on the Ritz!'
jokes.setup: Moira tells David the murder mystery party is Roaring Twenties themed and he should dress up
jokes.punchline: She delivers a string of authentic 1920s slang — 'dew-dropper,' 'peepers,' 'swell,' 'put on the Ritz' — with complete fluency and theatrical commitment
jokes.explanation: Only Moira Rose would naturally slip into 1920s slang as a motivational speech. The density of period-appropriate vocabulary ('dew-dropper' = lazy person, 'peepers' = eyes, 'swell' = an elegant person) delivered without explanation or irony is a great character moment. High character integration — this is one of those lines that could only come from Moira.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Is there any other way?' — responding to David's '...twice around the room, and then I'm out.'
jokes.setup: David sets his conditions for attending the party.
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'Is there any other way?' implies this is the correct, sophisticated way to attend parties — validating David's pretension.
jokes.explanation: Moira confirming David's affected party behavior as correct is a lovely mother-son bonding moment through shared pretension. Economy is excellent — five words that say everything about their shared worldview.

jokes.text: Moira's phone call during golf practice: 'Rose residence. / You know who this is. / May I ask who's calling?'
jokes.setup: Moira is interrupted by a phone call while watching Johnny putt
jokes.punchline: She answers with the full formal 'Rose residence' greeting — as if they still live in their mansion — then plays completely dumb when David calls
jokes.explanation: Moira saying 'Rose residence' when they live in a motel room is a small ongoing joke about her refusal to accept the reality of their circumstances. The power dynamic of then refusing to acknowledge David ('May I ask who's calling?') adds a second layer.

jokes.text: Moira on the phone to David: 'They'll never catch me.' — about being the murderer at Twyla's party
jokes.setup: David calls Moira from the party to tell her Twyla keeps asking about her — he suspects Moira is the murderer
jokes.punchline: Moira's dramatic 'They'll never catch me' — committing to the bit with full villain energy from her golf course
jokes.explanation: Three words, perfectly delivered. 'They'll never catch me' functions as both a game commitment (she literally is probably the murderer) and a genuine Moira character line — she's fully inhabiting the role with theatrical relish from a golf course. Maximum economy, very high quotability, strong character integration.

jokes.text: David: 'You know who this is, and you know where I am.' / Moira: 'Did everyone show up?' / David: 'Yes, everybody showed up.' / Moira: 'Good, then my work is done.'
jokes.setup: David is calling in apparent distress from the party Moira sent him to
jokes.punchline: Moira, having successfully engineered the party's attendance numbers, immediately declares her work done and disengages — mission accomplished, David's complaints irrelevant
jokes.explanation: Moira's immediate pivot to 'my work is done' upon hearing the party is full reveals her priorities — she was never invested in David or Twyla's enjoyment, only the optics of success. Good character beat.

jokes.text: The Rose parents discover Alexis didn't actually graduate: 'We were at the graduation!' / 'Yeah, but I wasn't.'
jokes.setup: The revelation that Alexis didn't finish high school leads her parents to insist they attended her graduation
jokes.punchline: Alexis calmly informs them she wasn't there — she missed her own graduation — delivered matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: A fantastic joke structure. The parents are indignant on her behalf ('We were at the graduation!') and she has to calmly inform them that she, the graduate, was not present. The implication — that the parents went, sat through a graduation, and never noticed Alexis wasn't there — is the devastating and hilarious kicker. Strong craft scores across the board; one of the episode's best moments.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'We might not have stayed through the "R's," I think we left halfway through the "L's."'
jokes.setup: Johnny insists he saw Alexis at graduation; pushed for details, he admits they left before the end
jokes.punchline: They left so early (halfway through the L's) that they could not possibly have seen her — Rose would be near the end of the alphabet — proving they weren't actually there for her
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's top two jokes. The logic is brilliant: Rose is near the end of the alphabet, so if they left at the L's, they definitely didn't see Alexis graduate. But they also didn't notice she wasn't there. The casual 'Oh yeah, it got very repetitive' from Moira is the perfect button — she's not even apologetic. This is excellent comedy writing: specific, escalating, revealing of character (the Roses' neglect of their children dressed as attendance), and structurally airtight.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Oh yeah, it got very repetitive' — referring to sitting through graduation names
jokes.setup: Johnny explains they left halfway through the L's because 'it got very repetitive'
jokes.punchline: Moira cheerfully agrees — graduation is, yes, repetitive — with no awareness that she's confessing to abandoning her daughter's graduation
jokes.explanation: The button on the graduation scene. Moira agreeing that graduation 'got very repetitive' is both a perfect Moira line (she's not wrong, it IS repetitive) and a devastating character confession wrapped in cheerfulness. Near-maximum economy — four words that do enormous work. The complete absence of guilt is what makes it funny rather than sad.

jokes.text: Moira: 'No, that was-that was me.' [confusing the spray tan run-in with herself rather than Alexis]
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to confirm memory of a celebratory lunch where Alexis had a spray tan incident as proof they saw her graduate.
jokes.punchline: Moira realizes the spray tan incident was her, not Alexis — their memories of their daughter's graduation are so vague they're substituting their own experiences.
jokes.explanation: The revelation that Moira can't distinguish between her own and her daughter's experiences is both funny and character-revealing. Johnny's enthusiastic memory ('Ah! Yeah!') once Moira corrects him is a great beat. The spray tan detail is wonderfully specific.

jokes.text: The graduation photo turns out to be Jess Chang, not Alexis
jokes.setup: Alexis's parents are still insisting they can prove they attended — Johnny produces a graduation photo from his wallet
jokes.punchline: The photo is of a completely different person ('That's Jess Chang!') — Johnny has been carrying the wrong graduation photo in his wallet
jokes.explanation: The episode's single best joke, and a genuinely great piece of sitcom writing. The escalating revelation has perfect structure: parents claim they attended → parents left early → parents show photo as 'proof' → photo is the wrong person. The final twist that Johnny doesn't even have a photo of Alexis in his wallet — just a random classmate — is the devastating culmination. Every component is character-perfect: Johnny's misplaced certainty, Moira's attempts to justify it ('well, who can tell with all this hair'), Alexis's 'That's not my face!' It's the kind of joke that reveals something true and slightly dark about these characters while being genuinely hilarious. This earns its high scores.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Then how do you explain this graduation photo?' — revealing a photo that is clearly not her
jokes.setup: Johnny and Moira claim they have memories of Alexis's graduation
jokes.punchline: Alexis produces a graduation photo as evidence — which turns out to be someone else entirely
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the visual punchline. Alexis wielding a photo as definitive proof, only for it to be the wrong person, is a clean structural comedy beat.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Well, who can tell with all this hair in front of your face?'
jokes.setup: Alexis points out the photo is of Jess Chang, not her
jokes.punchline: Moira attempts to defend the photo by suggesting it could be Alexis because of the hair — a completely inadequate justification
jokes.explanation: Moira's attempt to salvage the situation by claiming the hair makes it ambiguous is both a great character moment (she refuses to concede the point) and a very funny deflection. 'Who can tell with all this hair in front of your face' — said to someone who can clearly tell it's a different person — is perfectly Moira. Strong character integration and quotability.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'That's not my face!'
jokes.setup: Moira suggests the Jess Chang photo might be Alexis due to the hair
jokes.punchline: 'That's not my face!' — Alexis's indignant clarification that she can identify her own face
jokes.explanation: Three words, perfectly delivered. 'That's not my face!' is so obvious and yet the fact that it needs to be said is the joke. The directness of having to explicitly state that the photo is not of her is absurdly funny. Maximum economy. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Okay, why didn't anyone come to my graduation?' Johnny: 'Well, you didn't graduate in Switzerland.' Alexis: 'And whose fault is that?!'
jokes.setup: Alexis is upset that her parents apparently didn't attend her graduation
jokes.punchline: Johnny tries to deflect blame by noting she didn't graduate in Switzerland (the planned location). Alexis turns it back: 'And whose fault is that?!' — she didn't graduate because of something the parents presumably caused
jokes.explanation: The rapid exchange — Johnny deflecting with the Switzerland excuse, Alexis immediately firing back 'And whose fault is that?!' — is very well-structured. The implication that Alexis didn't graduate because of whatever chaos her wealthy parents created is left beautifully ambiguous, and the speed of the comeback gives it great comedic momentum.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'How long have you been carrying that around in your wallet?!'
jokes.setup: It's just been revealed Johnny had a photo he thought was Alexis's graduation but was actually Jess Chang's
jokes.punchline: Alexis turns the accusation on Johnny — the implication being that this stranger's photo has been in his wallet for years, possibly decades, without him noticing
jokes.explanation: A great button to the scene. The question implies Johnny has been confidently carrying Jess Chang's graduation photo as if it were his daughter's, for an indeterminate period, without ever noticing. The horror of that realization (and the fact that he apparently never once looked closely) is the final layer of the joke.

tmdb_overview: Johnny and Stevie pitch a nearby golf course on a package deal with the motel. Moira tries to get out of an invitation to Twyla’s murder mystery party.
insight: Murder mystery setup lets character quirks escalate naturally across fifty jokes.
